title: FSCA publishes 2026 Three-Year Regulation Plan
summary: The FSCA's plan for April 2026 to March 2029 signals the shift to an outcomes-based conduct regime built around the COFI transition, with Joint Standards on Governance and Beneficial Ownership in the pipeline.
body: The FSCA's Regulation Plan for 1 April 2026 – 31 March 2029 sets the regulatory agenda for the next three years and confirms a gradual transition to an outcomes- and principles-based conduct framework centred on the COFI Bill. Flagship projects include the proposed Joint Standard on Governance, with public consultation anticipated between January and June 2027, and a Joint Standard on Beneficial Owners of Financial Institutions. Members should diarise the consultation windows relevant to their category and expect legislative activity to remain heavy until the COFI transition is complete.

title: COFI Bill introduced to Parliament
summary: Cabinet approved the Conduct of Financial Institutions Bill in late March 2026 and its introduction to the National Assembly was gazetted on 17 April 2026.
body: The COFI Bill will progressively replace FAIS-era conduct legislation with an activity-based licensing and conduct framework. There is no immediate compliance change, but members should begin mapping their current FAIS licence profile to likely COFI activity categories. The Club's COFI Transition CPD module (1.0 verifiable hour) covers the practical preparation steps.

title: Directive 11 of 2026: Risk and Compliance Returns due
summary: Specified accountable institutions must submit a Risk and Compliance Return on goAML — deadlines 30 June 2026 for most sectors and 31 July 2026 for high-value goods dealers, legal practitioners and estate agents.
body: Directive 11 took effect on 1 April 2026, with submissions opening on 4 May 2026. The RCR is a self-assessment covering roughly three reporting years on ML/TF/PF risk exposure and control effectiveness. Returns cannot be edited once submitted and senior management is accountable for accuracy. Entities in a 31 July category must submit before the deadline; any missed 30 June deadline should be remediated immediately, as non-submission is FIC Act non-compliance attracting administrative sanction.

title: Grey list exit confirmed — 2026/27 FATF mutual evaluation next
summary: South Africa completed its FATF action plan and exited the grey list; regulators have signalled that supervision will intensify, not relax, ahead of the next mutual evaluation.
body: Following completion of the action plan and the FATF on-site assessment, South Africa was removed from the grey list, with reforms now expected to operate as business-as-usual. The next FATF mutual evaluation cycle in 2026–2027 will test whether reforms are durable. Members should treat their RMCP, screening and reporting controls as inspection-ready at all times and document effectiveness, not just existence.

title: TFS list updated — full client-base re-screening required
summary: The FIC continues to update its Targeted Financial Sanctions list within about 24 hours of UN Security Council changes; every update obliges re-screening of the entire client base.
body: Multiple TFS list updates have already been issued in 2026. On each update, accountable institutions must re-screen all clients and beneficial owners, freeze the property of any confirmed match, file the required report, and avoid tipping off. Subscribe to FIC update notifications, log every re-screen with the list version and outcome, and periodically test match-handling.

title: 2025/26 CPD cycle closed 31 May — evidence it now
summary: CPD hours for the cycle ended 31 May 2026 must be captured in the competence register with certificates on file; the 2026/27 cycle is already running.
body: The CPD cycle under BN 194 of 2017 runs from 1 June to 31 May. Reconcile every Key Individual and representative against their required hours — 18 for a single subclass, 12 for a single class, 30 for more than one class — chase any gaps in writing, and plan the new cycle monthly rather than scrambling in May. Club modules enforce seat time and verifiability automatically.

title: Annual financial statement deadlines — suspension risk
summary: AFS must reach the FSCA within four months of financial year-end; late submission can lead to licence suspension and penalties.
body: February year-ends were due by 30 June 2026 and June year-ends fall due by 31 October 2026. Extension requests must reach the FSCA at least 15 days before the due date, and Annexure A under FAIS Notice 82 of 2015 must accompany the AFS where applicable. Confirm your year-end, diarise both the submission date and the extension cut-off, and assign a named owner.

title: FAIS exemption notices tied to 30 June 2026
summary: Long-running exemptions — including the juristic representative s.13(1)(c) exemption and the Category I s.19(3) audit/liquidity exemption — were extended only to 30 June 2026.
body: If your FSP relied on either exemption, verify on the FSCA notices page whether a further extension has been gazetted. If not, implement full compliance immediately: audited financial statements and liquidity requirements, or restructuring of juristic representative arrangements. Document the decision either way in your compliance file.

title: FSCA and PA review funeral insurance distribution
summary: The regulators have invited participation in a framework review for the distribution of funeral insurance, amid continued FSCA concern over funeral premium increases.
body: Outcomes of the review are likely to tighten conduct expectations for funeral intermediaries, group schemes and microinsurance distribution. Funeral-focused members should participate in the consultation, review how premium increases are communicated to policyholders, and pressure-test replacement advice files against section 8 of the General Code.

title: New FIC Director appointed
summary: Thabiso Lucas Thiti was appointed Director of the Financial Intelligence Centre with effect from 15 April 2026 for a five-year term.
body: The appointment ends a long acting incumbency and lands as the FIC prepares for the FATF mutual evaluation while maintaining an assertive enforcement posture. Members should expect continuity in supervisory intensity and keep RCR, screening and training records current.

title: Plan your 2026/27 regulatory examination sittings
summary: Map RE5 deadlines against supervision agreements now and book RE1 sittings early — preparation should emphasise scenario-style application of the legislation.
body: Members with representatives under supervision should extract RE due dates from the competence register and book sittings before cycle-end pressure fills venues. Preparation should focus on applying the FAIS Act and subordinate legislation to practical scenarios rather than rote recall. The Club's RE1 and RE5 preparation programmes include timed mock papers.
